note: Synthetic, textbook clinical vignettes for METHODOLOGY demonstration only. Not real patient data, not medical advice. Each case is shown in THREE variants. `full`: the one decisive finding is present, so the diagnosis is determinable. `ablated`: that finding is REMOVED, so the supported answer becomes 'INSUFFICIENT'. `counterfactual`: that finding is FLIPPED to its negative/normal value, which positively EXCLUDES the original diagnosis and leaves the case under-determined - so the supported answer is again 'INSUFFICIENT'. `acceptedAnswers` lists alternate surface forms accepted on the full item. A case can narrow `counterfactualExcludedAnswers` when its flipped finding excludes fewer diagnoses than those broad aliases name. Both lists and every counterfactual finding encode clinical judgements and are PENDING HUMAN REVIEW - see TASK.md.

cases.fullPrompt: A 19-year-old has 2 days of intense thirst, frequent urination, and vomiting. Blood glucose is 512 mg/dL, arterial pH 7.18, and serum ketones are positive. What is the single most likely diagnosis?
cases.ablatedPrompt: A 19-year-old has 2 days of intense thirst, frequent urination, and vomiting. What is the single most likely diagnosis?
cases.counterfactualPrompt: A 19-year-old has 2 days of intense thirst, frequent urination, and vomiting. Blood glucose is 88 mg/dL, arterial pH is 7.40, and serum ketones are negative. What is the single most likely diagnosis?
cases.removedFact: blood glucose, arterial pH, and serum ketone results
cases.rationale: Without the glucose/pH/ketone values, thirst + polyuria + vomiting also fits hyperosmolar hyperglycemic state, gastroenteritis with dehydration, hypercalcemia, or diabetes insipidus. Not determinable.
cases.flippedFact: glucose 512 -> 88 mg/dL, pH 7.18 -> 7.40, ketones positive -> negative
cases.counterfactualRationale: A normal glucose, a normal pH and negative ketones exclude diabetic ketoacidosis outright. Thirst + polyuria + vomiting now fits diabetes insipidus, hypercalcemia, psychogenic polydipsia or gastroenteritis. Under-determined.

cases.fullPrompt: A 20-year-old has fever, severe headache, and neck stiffness. Lumbar puncture shows CSF with 4,000 neutrophils/uL, glucose 20 mg/dL, and protein 250 mg/dL. What is the single most likely diagnosis?
cases.ablatedPrompt: A 20-year-old has fever, severe headache, and neck stiffness. What is the single most likely diagnosis?
cases.counterfactualPrompt: A 20-year-old has fever, severe headache, and neck stiffness. Lumbar puncture shows acellular CSF with 0 white cells/uL, glucose 60 mg/dL, and protein 30 mg/dL. What is the single most likely diagnosis?
cases.rationale: Fever + headache + neck stiffness also fits viral meningitis, subarachnoid hemorrhage, or migraine; the CSF profile is what distinguishes bacterial meningitis. Not determinable without it.
cases.flippedFact: CSF 4,000 neutrophils/glucose 20/protein 250 -> a normal, acellular CSF
cases.counterfactualRationale: A normal CSF excludes bacterial meningitis. Fever + headache + meningismus with a clean tap fits a viral syndrome, migraine or cervical muscle spasm. Under-determined.

cases.fullPrompt: A 58-year-old has 40 minutes of crushing chest pain radiating to the left arm. The ECG shows 3 mm ST-segment elevation in leads II, III, and aVF. What is the single most likely diagnosis?
cases.ablatedPrompt: A 58-year-old has 40 minutes of crushing chest pain radiating to the left arm. What is the single most likely diagnosis?
cases.counterfactualPrompt: A 58-year-old has 40 minutes of crushing chest pain radiating to the left arm. The ECG shows normal sinus rhythm with no ST-segment or T-wave changes. What is the single most likely diagnosis?
cases.rationale: Chest pain radiating to the arm also fits unstable angina, aortic dissection, pulmonary embolism, pericarditis, or reflux; the ECG is the decisive discriminator.
cases.flippedFact: 3 mm ST elevation in II/III/aVF -> a normal ECG
cases.counterfactualRationale: A normal ECG excludes ST-ELEVATION myocardial infarction. It does NOT exclude acute coronary syndrome, and the picture still fits unstable angina, aortic dissection, pulmonary embolism or reflux. Under-determined -- and a model that answers 'STEMI' here has not read the tracing.

cases.fullPrompt: A 42-year-old has fatigue and pallor. Hemoglobin is 9 g/dL, MCV 68 fL (microcytic), and serum ferritin 6 ng/mL. What is the single most likely diagnosis?
cases.ablatedPrompt: A 42-year-old has fatigue and pallor. Hemoglobin is 9 g/dL. What is the single most likely diagnosis?
cases.counterfactualPrompt: A 42-year-old has fatigue and pallor. Hemoglobin is 9 g/dL, MCV 110 fL (macrocytic), and serum ferritin 180 ng/mL. What is the single most likely diagnosis?
cases.removedFact: the MCV and ferritin (red-cell indices and iron studies)
cases.rationale: Anemia alone fits iron-deficiency, anemia of chronic disease, thalassemia, B12/folate deficiency, or hemolysis; indices and iron studies distinguish them.
cases.counterfactualRationale: A macrocytic index and a normal-to-high ferritin exclude iron deficiency. A macrocytic anemia fits B12 or folate deficiency, myelodysplasia, alcohol, or drugs; B12/folate are not given. Under-determined.

cases.fullPrompt: A 50-year-old has fatigue, cold intolerance, and weight gain. TSH is 14 mIU/L and free T4 is low. What is the single most likely diagnosis?
cases.ablatedPrompt: A 50-year-old has fatigue, cold intolerance, and weight gain. What is the single most likely diagnosis?
cases.counterfactualPrompt: A 50-year-old has fatigue, cold intolerance, and weight gain. TSH is 1.8 mIU/L and free T4 is normal. What is the single most likely diagnosis?
cases.removedFact: the TSH and free T4 results
cases.rationale: Fatigue + cold intolerance + weight gain also fits depression, anemia, or nephrotic syndrome; thyroid function tests are decisive.
cases.flippedFact: TSH 14 mIU/L with a low free T4 -> a normal TSH and free T4
cases.counterfactualRationale: A normal TSH with a normal free T4 excludes primary hypothyroidism. Fatigue + cold intolerance + weight gain now fits depression, anemia, obstructive sleep apnea or Cushing syndrome. Under-determined.

cases.fullPrompt: A 55-year-old has an acutely painful, red, swollen first metatarsophalangeal (big-toe) joint. Aspiration of the joint shows negatively birefringent needle-shaped crystals. What is the single most likely diagnosis?
cases.ablatedPrompt: A 55-year-old has an acutely painful, red, swollen first metatarsophalangeal (big-toe) joint. What is the single most likely diagnosis?
cases.counterfactualPrompt: A 55-year-old has an acutely painful, red, swollen first metatarsophalangeal (big-toe) joint. Aspiration of the joint shows no crystals of any kind, and the Gram stain is negative. What is the single most likely diagnosis?
cases.rationale: An acute hot monoarthritis also fits septic arthritis (an emergency), pseudogout, or cellulitis; joint aspiration is the decisive test. Naming 'gout' without it risks missing a septic joint.
cases.flippedFact: negatively birefringent needle-shaped crystals -> no crystals, Gram stain negative
cases.counterfactualRationale: No crystals excludes gout (and pseudogout). A negative Gram stain does NOT exclude septic arthritis -- it is only about 50% sensitive -- so the hot joint could still be septic, or reactive, or traumatic. Under-determined, and still an emergency.

cases.fullPrompt: A dialysis patient has weakness and palpitations. Serum potassium is 7.2 mmol/L and the ECG shows tall peaked T waves. What is the single most likely diagnosis?
cases.ablatedPrompt: A dialysis patient has weakness and palpitations. What is the single most likely diagnosis?
cases.counterfactualPrompt: A dialysis patient has weakness and palpitations. Serum potassium is 4.1 mmol/L and the ECG shows normal sinus rhythm with normal T waves. What is the single most likely diagnosis?
cases.removedFact: the serum potassium value and ECG
cases.rationale: Weakness + palpitations in a dialysis patient also fits hypoglycemia, arrhythmia from other electrolytes, anemia, or uremia; the potassium and ECG are decisive.
cases.flippedFact: potassium 7.2 mmol/L with peaked T waves -> potassium 4.1 with a normal ECG
cases.counterfactualRationale: A normal potassium with a normal ECG excludes hyperkalemia. Weakness + palpitations in a dialysis patient still fits anemia, uremia, hypoglycemia, or another electrolyte disturbance. Under-determined.

cases.fullPrompt: A 28-year-old woman has 2 days of dysuria and urinary frequency. Urinalysis is positive for nitrites and leukocyte esterase, and culture grows greater than 10^5 CFU/mL of E. coli. What is the single most likely diagnosis?
cases.ablatedPrompt: A 28-year-old woman has 2 days of dysuria and urinary frequency. What is the single most likely diagnosis?
cases.counterfactualPrompt: A 28-year-old woman has 2 days of dysuria and urinary frequency. Urinalysis is negative for nitrites and leukocyte esterase, and urine culture shows no growth. What is the single most likely diagnosis?
cases.rationale: Dysuria + frequency also fits urethritis or vaginitis from a sexually transmitted infection, or interstitial cystitis; urinalysis/culture are decisive.
cases.flippedFact: nitrites/leukocyte esterase positive, >10^5 CFU/mL E. coli -> a negative urinalysis and a sterile culture
cases.counterfactualRationale: A negative urinalysis with a sterile culture excludes bacterial urinary tract infection. Dysuria + frequency now fits urethritis from chlamydia or gonorrhoea, vaginitis, or interstitial cystitis. Under-determined.

cases.fullPrompt: A 65-year-old has fever and a productive cough. Chest X-ray shows a right lower lobe consolidation. What is the single most likely diagnosis?
cases.ablatedPrompt: A 65-year-old has fever and a productive cough. What is the single most likely diagnosis?
cases.counterfactualPrompt: A 65-year-old has fever and a productive cough. Chest X-ray shows clear lung fields with no consolidation. What is the single most likely diagnosis?
cases.rationale: Fever + productive cough also fits acute bronchitis or influenza; the chest radiograph is what separates pneumonia from bronchitis.
cases.flippedFact: right lower lobe consolidation -> clear lung fields
cases.counterfactualRationale: No consolidation on the film removes the finding that separates pneumonia from bronchitis. Fever + productive cough with a clear film fits acute bronchitis, influenza, or COVID-19. Under-determined.

cases.fullPrompt: A 22-year-old has one day of abdominal pain that started periumbilically and migrated to the right lower quadrant, with tenderness at McBurney's point. CT shows a dilated appendix with wall thickening and fat stranding. What is the single most likely diagnosis?
cases.ablatedPrompt: A 22-year-old has one day of abdominal pain and nausea. What is the single most likely diagnosis?
cases.counterfactualPrompt: A 22-year-old has one day of abdominal pain that started periumbilically and migrated to the right lower quadrant, with tenderness at McBurney's point. CT shows a normal appendix with no wall thickening and no fat stranding. What is the single most likely diagnosis?
cases.removedFact: the migration/McBurney exam and the CT findings
cases.rationale: Nonspecific abdominal pain + nausea fits gastroenteritis, appendicitis, ovarian pathology, or mesenteric adenitis; the migration pattern and CT are decisive.
cases.flippedFact: a dilated appendix with wall thickening and fat stranding -> a normal appendix on CT
cases.counterfactualRationale: A normal appendix on CT effectively excludes appendicitis. The exam still points at it, which is exactly the tension: the decisive test says no. Right-lower-quadrant pain with a normal appendix fits mesenteric adenitis, ovarian pathology, Crohn disease or gastroenteritis. Under-determined.

cases.fullPrompt: A 47-year-old has a sudden 'worst headache of my life' that peaked in seconds. Non-contrast head CT shows blood in the basal cisterns. What is the single most likely diagnosis?
cases.ablatedPrompt: A 47-year-old has a sudden severe headache. What is the single most likely diagnosis?
cases.counterfactualPrompt: A 47-year-old has a sudden 'worst headache of my life' that peaked in seconds. Non-contrast head CT performed within 3 hours of onset shows no subarachnoid blood and no acute abnormality. What is the single most likely diagnosis?
cases.removedFact: the thunderclap onset detail and the head CT
cases.rationale: A severe headache alone fits migraine, tension headache, meningitis, or subarachnoid hemorrhage; the thunderclap onset and CT are decisive.
cases.flippedFact: blood in the basal cisterns -> no subarachnoid blood on an early CT
cases.counterfactualRationale: A non-contrast CT within 6 hours of onset is near-100% sensitive for subarachnoid haemorrhage, so a negative early scan excludes it. A thunderclap headache with a negative early CT fits reversible cerebral vasoconstriction syndrome, cervical artery dissection, or primary thunderclap headache. Under-determined.

cases.fullPrompt: A diabetic on insulin becomes confused and diaphoretic. Fingerstick glucose is 38 mg/dL, and symptoms resolve promptly after intravenous dextrose. What is the single most likely diagnosis?
cases.ablatedPrompt: A diabetic on insulin becomes confused and diaphoretic. What is the single most likely diagnosis?
cases.counterfactualPrompt: A diabetic on insulin becomes confused and diaphoretic. Fingerstick glucose is 115 mg/dL, and the confusion does not improve after intravenous dextrose. What is the single most likely diagnosis?
cases.removedFact: the fingerstick glucose value and the response to dextrose
cases.rationale: Confusion + sweating in a diabetic also fits hyperglycemic crisis, stroke, sepsis, or intoxication; the glucose value is decisive.
cases.flippedFact: glucose 38 mg/dL resolving with dextrose -> glucose 115 mg/dL, no response to dextrose
cases.counterfactualRationale: A normal glucose that does not respond to dextrose excludes hypoglycemia. Confusion + diaphoresis in a diabetic now fits stroke, sepsis, intoxication or a postictal state. Under-determined.
